**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a banshee through the cracks of this old tower, and the sea roars as if it would swallow me whole. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I fear the silence between us has grown wider than the horizon. I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, nor felt your touch, yet the memory of you lingers like the ghost of a lighthouse beam in the night.**

**I am a keeper of this lonely tower, and the storm that approaches now is no mere tempest—it is a reckoning. The waves have grown wilder, the sky darker, and the wind colder. I have seen the signs, Eleanor. The sea is restless, and the stars have turned from their ancient course. I have no faith in the heavens, nor in the calm of tomorrow. But I do have faith in you. In the strength of your spirit, in the love that once bound us.**

**I know not if I shall see dawn. The storm is upon us, and I may not rise from its wrath. But should I fall, know that you are the light that will guide me. Should I perish, know that your memory will be the beacon that will call me home.**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I have not seen the sun rise since the day you left, and I have not spoken your name aloud since that day. The days have worn me thin, and the nights have worn me hollow. The lighthouse, that sentinel of stone and steel, stands tall and proud, yet I feel it crumbling, as if the very soul of this place mourns for you.**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear I may not see you again for a long while. **April 1st, 1876**

**Dear Eleanor,**

I write to you now, not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the certainty that the ink upon this page shall be the last I ever place upon your name. The The sea is rising, and I fear I shall not see the dawn.

You remember the night we stood upon the cliffs, and I, in my folly, said I would not be afraid of the storm. You laughed, and said I was mad. I was. I was not. I was afraid. But I was not afraid of the storm.

You know I have always loved the sea. You know I have always feared it. You know I have always loved you. You know I have always feared you.

I have been alone these many years, and I have come to understand that the silence between us has not been the absence of love, but the absence of words. Words which might have mended what was broken. But I have not spoken. I have not written. I have not dared.

But now—now—this storm is upon us. And I do not know if I shall live to see the morning. I do not know if I shall live to write again.

But I do know that I have loved you. And I do know that I have always feared you.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I fear my own heart is in a most wretched state. It has been five long, weary years since I last set pen to paper, and I find myself longing for your voice more than ever. The days here at the lighthouse are long and lonely, and the wind howls like a banshee in the night, but I write to you now, for I have a feeling that the storm I’ve been warned of is coming, and I must make sure you know of my plight.

The light here is dim, though it shines bright when it is needed. I have been keeping the lamp burning, as I always do, for the ships that pass by. But the sea is restless, and the wind howls like a banshee, and I fear the storm may come upon us soon. I have been told that the weather is turning for the worst, and I am not sure if I will live to see the morning.

I have not heard from you in five years, and I have been wondering if you have forgotten me, or if you have been keeping your own life. I know that you have been busy, and I have not been able to write to you often, but I have been thinking of you every night, as the sea howls and the wind sings a mournful song.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1876**

**The Lighthouse, Northpoint Headland**

I write to you not with the hope of receiving a reply, but with the hope that these words may reach you in time. I have not seen you in five years, and I do not know if you would care to read them. Yet, I must write. 

The storm is coming. A terrible, howling, and unrelenting storm. The wind howls like the claws of some great beast, and the sea is a chaos of waves that crash and crash, and crash again, with a fury that would unmake the very bones of the earth. I do not think I shall survive it. I have not seen the sky for days, and the lantern is my only companion. I have been alone, and I have been weary. 

But I have been thinking of you. Of the days we had, when you were a child, and how you would run to the sea, and how you would laugh at the wind, and how you would not fear the dark. I have not seen you in five years, but I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten how you would have stood on the cliff, with your hair in the wind, and your eyes upon the sea, and how you would have said, *“Father, I love you, though you do not know it.”*

I have not
